title: The Half-Second Before
description: Harmonia, ch.1. An old woman at the well is carrying a hurt that is older than the scrape she is holding. Cleric's solo duel — outlast, not defeat. The party cannot help with this. They watch.

steps.text: An old woman sat on the edge of the well, holding her shin. A scrape — not deep, but the wrong shape of dark, and the kind of fall that doesn't stop being a fall when you stand up.

The Cleric knelt beside her before the party had finished registering the scene. She looked at the shin. She looked at the shin the way you look at something that is only partly the thing you are looking at.

lines.speaker: Cleric
lines.text: How long have you been carrying this?

lines.speaker: Cleric
lines.text: Not the scrape. The other thing.

steps.text: The old woman did not answer. The old woman had, it turned out, been the answer for a long time.

cleric.text: The lead was already reaching. They had known before the woman said anything. This is what the lead does when a room contains a hurt that has not been named.

lines.speaker: Mage
lines.text: I don't know. I don't have language for it.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: Then be quiet. Watch her hands.

default.text: The Cleric put her hands on the old woman's hands. Not on the shin. On the hands that were holding the shin.

steps.text: For half a second she paused. The kind of half-second nobody would have noticed if they hadn't been looking at her hands. It was not hesitation exactly — more like a small door she had to open before the rest could happen. She opened the door. She always opened the door.

The party could not help her with this. The party could not, in fact, see what she was fighting. They only saw her hands, and the old woman's breath, and the way the light around them seemed for a moment to be waiting.
The Cleric exhaled once. Something in the room — something the party could not see but had been feeling, without knowing they were feeling it, since they sat down — let go.
The old woman opened her eyes. She had not, the party realized, been looking at anything for some time.

lines.speaker: Cleric
lines.text: Walk on it tomorrow. Today, sit.

lines.speaker: Cleric
lines.text: Tell me if it comes back. I'll come back.

steps.text: She meant it. She would come back. The Cleric was, the party would learn, the kind of person who said things and then did them — and the kind of person who, when the door needed opening, opened the door. Every time. Even when the door cost her something the party had not yet learned to notice.
